attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if SpaceX completed at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC. The cleanest reference class from the fixed packet is an organization that has just set a major annual launch-cadence record and needs only modest additional growth the following year. SpaceX had 31, 61, and 96 launches in 2021-2023, so 100 in 2024 requires about 4.2% growth after much larger recent increases. A base-rate estimate around 60% seems appropriate because sustaining record cadence is materially hard, but the required growth over the prior year is small. Case-specific evidence moves this upward: operational cadence was already near twice weekly by late 2023, most missions were repeatable Falcon 9/Starlink launches rather than entirely customer-dependent one-offs, and the company had publicly discussed very high cadence ambitions. I do not move higher than the low-to-mid 70s because a single Falcon 9 grounding, range bottleneck, pad constraint, weather cluster, or operational distraction could erase the small margin above 100. Final probability: 72%.
attempts.strongest_yes: The 100-launch threshold is barely above the 96 launches already achieved in 2023, and late-2023 cadence was already near the needed run rate.
attempts.strongest_no: A short Falcon 9 grounding or several operational bottlenecks could easily cost more than four launches, turning a near-threshold year into a miss.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether Falcon 9 remains continuously available without a material grounding.","Whether late-2023 near-twice-weekly cadence can be sustained across the whole year.","Whether range, pad, weather, and recovery operations scale smoothly enough.","How much Starlink demand and readiness continue to fill the manifest.","Whether Starship work competes with Falcon launch execution."]
attempts.premortem: If the forecast resolves false, the most likely story is not that 100 was intrinsically unreachable, but that a disruption erased the narrow margin: a Falcon 9 issue caused a grounding, range or pad throughput hit a ceiling, weather created repeated slips, or manifest gaps/customer delays left SpaceX a few launches short despite strong baseline demand.
attempts.wildcards: ["Major Falcon 9 anomaly or investigation grounding.","Unexpected increase or decrease in Starlink deployment urgency.","Range or pad policy/availability changes.","Severe weather clusters affecting key launch sites.","Operational prioritization shift toward Starship."]
attempts.cited_sources: [{"claim":"Fixed evidence packet states SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023 after 61 in 2022 and 31 in 2021, with most launches supporting Starlink and late-2023 cadence near twice weekly."}]

attempts.resolution_boundary: True if SpaceX completed 100 or more orbital launches between 2024-01-01 00:00 UTC and 2024-12-31 23:59 UTC; false otherwise. Suborbital activity or launches outside calendar year 2024 UTC do not count.
attempts.evidence_for: ["SpaceX completed 96 orbital launches in 2023, so the threshold requires only 4 additional launches, about 4.2% growth.","Recent annual cadence rose sharply: 31 in 2021, 61 in 2022, and 96 in 2023.","Operational cadence was already near twice weekly by late 2023, roughly consistent with a 100-plus annual pace if sustained.","Most launches supported Starlink, suggesting a large internal demand source and repeatable mission profile.","The company had publicly discussed very high launch-cadence ambitions."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true requires SpaceX to complete at least 100 orbital launches from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31 UTC; suborbital tests or launches after 2024-12-31 UTC do not count. Reference class: an already record-setting launch operator that ended the prior year just below a round-number threshold and needs less than 5% annual growth while operating a repeatable internal-demand-heavy cadence. I start around 65% because sustaining record industrial cadence is hard, but a 4-launch increase over 96 is a small threshold distance. Case-specific updates: +8 points for the strong 31 to 61 to 96 growth trend, +5 for late-2023 cadence already near the needed twice-weekly level, +4 for Starlink providing elastic internal launch demand, and -4 for grounding/range/weather/pad/customer-delay risks that can remove several launch opportunities quickly. Net inside-view estimate is 78%. The main reason this is not higher is that the question requires a full-year sustained operational record with little tolerance for multi-week disruptions, even though the required growth over 2023 is modest.
attempts.strongest_yes: The threshold is barely above 2023 performance: after reaching 96 launches and ending 2023 near twice-weekly cadence, SpaceX only needs to avoid a material disruption and sustain roughly the same system with modest improvement.
attempts.strongest_no: A single serious Falcon 9 grounding, pad/range bottleneck, or weather-heavy period could cost multiple launch slots, and at this record cadence there is not much slack if several risks cluster.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How much schedule margin SpaceX has above exactly 100 planned launches.","Whether Falcon 9 reliability remains uninterrupted through the year.","Whether range, weather, and pad turnaround constraints can support sustained near-twice-weekly operations.","How much Starlink launch demand remains ready to absorb schedule gaps.","Whether Starship work meaningfully diverts attention from Falcon 9 operations."]
attempts.premortem: If this resolves false, the most likely story is not lack of ambition but operational interruption: Falcon 9 is grounded for a period, or range/weather/pad constraints compound with customer delays, leaving SpaceX at something like 90 to 99 launches despite strong baseline cadence. Another false path is that late-2023 cadence proves temporarily stretched rather than a sustainable full-year rate.

A base rate around 60% seems appropriate: modest growth after a record year is common when capacity is expanding, but sustaining record cadence across a full year is fragile. The inside view is stronger because 96 launches in 2023 is only four short of the threshold, the 2021-2023 trajectory was steeply upward, most launches were internally driven Starlink missions, and late-2023 cadence was already near the required rate. I do not push this above the low 70s because a single meaningful Falcon 9 grounding, pad/range bottleneck, or operational pause could erase the small margin above 100.
attempts.strongest_yes: The cleanest argument for yes is that 96 launches in 2023 puts SpaceX almost at the target already, with a steep multi-year growth trend and late-2023 cadence near or above the required annual rate.
attempts.strongest_no: The cleanest argument for no is that almost the entire forecast depends on a single operational stack continuing without a serious grounding or bottleneck; one multi-week interruption could turn a near-certain-looking modest increase into a miss.

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if UK general election polling day occurs from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31, and false if polling day is delayed into January 2025 or later. Reference class: elections in parliamentary systems where the incumbent has discretion but is entering the last calendar year before a hard legal deadline. I estimate the base rate around 80% for holding the election before the final possible month, because governments normally preserve scheduling flexibility and avoid cliff-edge timing. The case-specific evidence pushes upward: the 2019 Parliament had to face an election by January 2025, a 2024 election was broadly expected, spring/autumn/late-2024 were live options, and a false outcome required using the latest possible timetable. The main counterweight is that the incumbent Conservatives were badly trailing, which could create an incentive to wait as long as legally possible. Overall, the legal deadline plus expectation of a 2024 election dominate, but I leave meaningful probability for strategic delay into January 2025.
attempts.strongest_yes: The election was legally due by January 2025, and the evidence packet says a 2024 election was broadly expected while a non-2024 outcome required pushing to the latest possible timetable.
attempts.strongest_no: A badly trailing incumbent with discretion could rationally delay to the legal limit if it believed economic or political conditions might improve before the deadline.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How much practical flexibility the Prime Minister had to choose January 2025 rather than late 2024.","Whether poor polling made delay more attractive or instead made earlier timing preferable to avoid further deterioration.","Whether political, administrative, or party-management constraints made a very late 2024 election more likely than January 2025."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast is wrong, the likely reason is that I overweighted the broad expectation of a 2024 election and underweighted the incumbent's incentive to delay while trailing badly. The government may have judged that waiting until the latest possible timetable was its best available option despite reduced flexibility.
attempts.wildcards: ["Major political crisis forcing an earlier election.","Sudden improvement or deterioration in Conservative polling changing timing incentives.","Administrative or legal interpretation of the final permissible polling date making January 2025 more or less feasible."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: true only if UK general election polling day occurs between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31 inclusive; false if polling day is in January 2025 or later. The concrete timeline strongly favors 2024 because the December 2019 Parliament must face an election by January 2025, leaving 2024 as almost the entire remaining legal window. I start from a reference class of elected governments near the end of a maximum parliamentary term with discretion over timing and a hard deadline early in the following year; most would avoid compressing the decision into the latest legally possible timetable, giving an estimated base rate of about 85% for the election falling in the preceding calendar year. Case-specific evidence moves this modestly upward: the evidence packet says a 2024 election was broadly expected, possible timing windows existed across spring, autumn, and very late 2024, and avoiding 2024 required using the latest possible timetable. The main offset is that the Conservatives were trailing Labour badly, so the prime minister had an incentive to delay in hope of improved conditions. That delay incentive prevents pushing the forecast into the mid/high 90s, but it mostly shifts timing within 2024 toward autumn or late 2024 rather than making January 2025 the central case.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is the threshold distance: almost the entire remaining legal window is in 2024, and the evidence packet explicitly says a non-2024 outcome required using the latest possible timetable, while 2024 had several plausible timing windows.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is incumbent incentive under bad polling: a government trailing badly may rationally spend every available week hoping for recovery, making January 2025 a live possibility despite being the latest and least flexible route.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["How strongly the prime minister would value optionality and orderly timing versus maximizing delay under poor polling.","Whether late-2023 polling deficits were seen as recoverable enough to justify pushing close to the legal limit.","Whether political, administrative, or parliamentary events would make a spring, autumn, or late-2024 election more or less attractive.","How much weight to place on the packet's statement that a 2024 election was broadly expected, since no independent source detail is available."]
attempts.premortem: If this forecast resolves false, the likely story is that the Conservatives' polling position stayed poor enough that the prime minister judged there was little downside to waiting until the latest possible timetable, hoping for economic or political improvement and accepting the flexibility risk. I would have underestimated how much a badly trailing incumbent prefers maximum delay over schedule control.

The base rate is high because governments approaching the end of a parliamentary term usually avoid using the absolute latest possible timetable unless there is a strong strategic reason. The fixed evidence pushes higher: the 2019 Parliament had to face an election by January 2025, a 2024 election was broadly expected, and avoiding 2024 required choosing the latest possible path. attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true if the FOMC lowers the federal funds target range at least once between 2024-01-01 and 2024-12-31, including scheduled or unscheduled meetings. My reference class is calendar years following an aggressive tightening cycle where policy is already restrictive, inflation is falling from a peak, and central bank projections or communications have shifted toward eventual easing. I estimate that base rate at about 65%, because once policy reaches a high restrictive plateau after a rapid hiking cycle, at least one cut within the following year is more likely than not, but not automatic. The fixed evidence pushes above that: the December 2023 FOMC projections implied multiple 2024 cuts conditional on continued disinflation, inflation had already declined substantially, and market pricing plus Fed communications had shifted toward cuts as a plausible base case. The main offset is that inflation remained above target and resilient labor markets could delay or prevent cuts if inflation reaccelerated. Net, at least one cut during the full calendar year looks substantially more likely than not, though timing and inflation persistence keep this below very high confidence.

attempts.strongest_no: Inflation was still above target and resilient labor markets could keep inflation pressure high enough that the Fed holds rates unchanged throughout 2024.

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if a major USD spot market price for Bitcoin exceeds $100,000 at least once after 2024-01-01 and before 2025-01-01. I use a reference class of Bitcoin years or cycle windows where price begins the year after a strong recovery, an important bullish catalyst is expected, and the target requires roughly a doubling within the next 12 months. The base rate is materially below even odds because Bitcoin can double in bull phases, but failed breakouts and deep drawdowns are common, and needing a new six-figure level from the mid-$40,000s is a high bar. I start around 30%. Case-specific evidence moves this upward: anticipated US spot Bitcoin ETF approvals could create a new demand channel, the April 2024 halving gives a historically bullish narrative, and prior cycles have produced large rallies. But much of the ETF optimism may already be reflected after the strong 2023 recovery, and the packet says $100,000 likely requires several favorable conditions at once: ETF inflows, macro support, and sustained risk appetite. Net, I update modestly upward to 36%, not to a majority.
attempts.strongest_yes: The combination of widely anticipated spot ETF approvals, an April halving, and existing positive momentum creates a plausible setup for a cycle-style rally large enough to more than double Bitcoin within 2024.
attempts.strongest_no: More than doubling from the mid-$40,000s in less than a year requires unusually strong and sustained demand, while the same historical cycle evidence also includes frequent drawdowns and failed breakouts.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether spot Bitcoin ETF approvals occur and generate sustained net inflows rather than a short-lived sell-the-news reaction.","Whether macro and liquidity conditions support risk assets through 2024.","Whether the halving narrative produces actual incremental buying during 2024 or only later.","Whether starting near the mid-$40,000s after a strong recovery means the bullish catalysts are already partly priced in."]
attempts.premortem: If the forecast is wrong on the low side, the likely failure mode is underweighting reflexive ETF inflows and a halving-driven momentum cycle that rapidly pushes Bitcoin into a new all-time-high regime. If the forecast is wrong on the high side, the likely failure mode is treating anticipated catalysts as additive when they were already priced in, followed by macro weakness, weak ETF flows, or a major drawdown before Bitcoin can approach $100,000.
attempts.wildcards: ["Exceptionally large institutional ETF inflows soon after approval.","A broad liquidity-driven risk rally across speculative assets.","A severe crypto market shock, exchange failure, regulatory surprise, or macro tightening episode.","A fast speculative mania once Bitcoin approaches prior highs."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if a major USD spot Bitcoin market prints above $100,000 at any time from 2024-01-01 through 2024-12-31, not merely if Bitcoin rallies strongly or approaches the level. My concrete reference class is Bitcoin calendar-year periods starting after a large recovery, with a known bullish catalyst ahead, where the target requires roughly a 2.2x move from the start-of-year price. A neutral base rate for clearing such an ambitious round-number threshold within one year is about 25%: Bitcoin has historically had explosive years, but after strong recoveries many years also stall, retrace, or fail to extend enough. I update upward for two unusually concrete 2024 catalysts: anticipated US spot ETF approvals, which could create a new institutional demand channel, and the expected April halving, which historically has supported cycle narratives and supply-tightening expectations. I update downward because the move requires more than doubling from the mid-$40,000s, the ETF approval was widely anticipated and therefore partly priced, halving effects may lag rather than occur within the same calendar year, and sustained macro risk appetite would likely be required. Net effect: base 25%, plus about 10 points for ETF-flow mechanism, plus 6 points for halving/cycle reflexivity, minus 3 points for prior 2023 run-up and partial anticipation, yielding about 38%.

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if Starliner's first crewed flight both launches with astronauts and returns those same astronauts to Earth before 2025-01-01. I use the reference class of late-stage human-spaceflight certification test missions that are publicly targeting launch in the coming calendar year after multiple prior delays. A rough base rate for completing the targeted crewed test and nominal return within that year is about 60%, because institutional programs often eventually fly once they are at public target-date stage, but first-crewed-flight schedules commonly slip. The case-specific evidence pulls in both directions: NASA/Boeing were targeting a 2024 crewed ISS launch and had strong institutional pressure to certify, but the program's history of software, valve, parachute, and certification issues raises the chance of another delay or nonstandard return plan. Net, I stay close to the base rate but shave it slightly for accumulated technical surprises and safety constraints.
attempts.strongest_yes: The strongest yes argument is that by early 2024 the program was already in late-stage public planning for a crewed ISS test, with NASA and Boeing institutionally motivated to complete certification and an expected short mission profile that should normally return before year-end if launched in 2024.
attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that this exact program had repeatedly failed to meet prior timelines because of varied technical and certification problems, so a 2024 target could easily slip or encounter a crew-safety issue that prevents a before-2025 return.
attempts.key_uncertainties: ["Whether remaining certification issues are genuinely close to closure as of 2024-01-01.","Whether the 2024 target has schedule margin or is mostly aspirational.","Whether any new technical surprise appears after crew launch that changes return timing.","How much to discount public planning assumptions for a program with repeated prior delays."]

attempts.rationale: Resolution boundary: this resolves true only if the first crewed Boeing Starliner flight both launches with astronauts and returns those same astronauts to Earth before 2025-01-01. I use as reference class delayed first crewed spacecraft certification/test flights that are publicly targeted for the coming year but still carry unresolved certification and safety risk. A rough base rate is 55%: many such programs eventually fly when institutional pressure and public schedules converge, but first-crewed-flight schedules are fragile. Updating from that base: the explicit 2024 NASA/Boeing target, ISS mission plan, institutional pressure to complete certification, and public assumption of a short crewed test add about 15 points. The years of software, valve, parachute, and certification delays subtract about 10 points; the possibility that crew safety constraints cause either a launch slip or altered return plan subtracts another 2 points net after accounting for overlap. The resulting inside-view probability is 58%. The event is conjunctive: launch must occur in 2024 and return before year-end, so even a successful launch late in 2024, an extended mission, or a safety-driven return change can make it false.

attempts.strongest_no: The strongest no argument is that Starliner had a repeated history of late-discovered technical and certification problems, and crewed test flights are safety-gated; one more major issue could slip launch beyond 2024 or turn an expected short mission into one that fails the before-2025 return condition.

The base reference class is large automakers or high-growth vehicle manufacturers already at roughly 1.5 million to 2 million annual units attempting another double-digit delivery increase without a major new mass-market model launch. A rough base rate for clearing a required 10% unit growth threshold in that situation is around 45%. Tesla starts from a strong 2023 base of about 1.81 million deliveries, so the required growth is not extreme relative to its prior rapid growth and capacity exists. But the evidence packet points to demand, pricing, high interest rates, competition, and lack of a new mass-market platform as central constraints. Cybertruck is beginning production but is not expected to contribute large near-term volume. I therefore update downward from the base rate to 39%, because capacity makes the target plausible but the demand-side evidence makes a miss somewhat more likely than a hit.

attempts.reference_class: Recent Olympic men's basketball tournaments in which the United States entered with the world's deepest talent pool and was expected to contend strongly for gold, especially 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020 as described in the fixed evidence packet.
attempts.resolution_boundary: Resolve true if, and only if, the United States men's basketball team won the men's basketball gold medal at the Paris 2024 Olympic Games. Other medals, pre-tournament roster quality, or performance in non-Olympic FIBA events do not satisfy resolution.
attempts.evidence_for: ["The United States had won men's Olympic basketball gold in 2008, 2012, 2016, and 2020.","The US talent pool remained the deepest in the world.","Early signals suggested a star-heavy US roster was likely for Paris after disappointment at the 2023 World Cup."]
attempts.evidence_against: ["Recent FIBA tournaments showed increased international parity and vulnerability when top US stars did not play.","France, Serbia, Canada, Germany, and others had NBA-level talent.","US gold probability depended heavily on roster commitment, health, and single-elimination variance."]
attempts.calibration_warnings: ["Base rate is based on a very small and highly selected recent Olympic sample.","The fixed evidence packet gives qualitative signals but no market odds, roster list, Elo-style ratings, or player availability probabilities.","Assumptions about star commitment, health, and team quality are correlated; if one is wrong, several supporting arguments weaken together.","Recent US Olympic dominance may anchor the forecast too high despite increased international parity.","Avoiding post-cutoff knowledge is important; this forecast uses only the fixed packet and not the actual tournament outcome."]
